title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Bacon and Credlock discuss the possibility that the killer mistakenly targeted Alfred with poison intended for an older man, noting that the poison likely entered his body through tea, even though the utensils had been professionally cleaned. Credlock later meets with an exhausted Lucy, suggesting that arsenic was mixed into the curry she prepared for Mr. Crickenshaw. Lucy notes that several people had access to the kitchen while she was setting the table, including Mr. Crickenshaw, Emma, Cecil, Harold, Alfred, and Brian Eastley, though Eastley had already left for Bramhampton.

Credlock muses on the lack of a clear motive, questioning the connection between Alfred's poisoning and the death of Matilda, Edmund's widow, in a sarcophagus. He warns Lucy that a poisoner remains in the house and that someone upstairs is faking a less severe poisoning. Lucy subsequently visits Mr. Cruckes, who is recovering and complaining about the nurses. While Mr. Cruckes blames the illness on mushrooms in the soup, he reveals his satisfaction regarding Alfred's death, stating that Alfred will no longer bother him or receive any money. This comment prompts Lucy to research the concept of a "Tontine pension."

Meanwhile, Crocker and Clutter consult Dr. Morris regarding the suspected arsenic poisoning. Dr. Morris, the former family doctor to Jossiah Crickenshaw, dismisses the idea of widespread poisoning but admits he did not previously suspect arsenic in Luther Crickenshaw’s stomach ailments. Regarding the family's mental stability, Dr. Morris explains that while Luther's mother was a mental patient, Luther is merely grouchy, though he harbors a deep, obsessive dislike for his son.
